title: Big News: Justice Department Drops Investigation Into Fed Chair
summary: Have you ever wondered what happens when the government investigates someone important? Well, the Justice Department just dropped its criminal investigation into Jerome Powell, the chair of the Federal Reserve (the group that helps manage the U.S. economy). They were looking into whether he lied to Congress about renovations at the Fed's headquarters. Instead, the Fed's inspector general will check on the cost overruns. This opens the door for Kevin Warsh to be confirmed as the next Fed chair. Senator Thom Tillis had threatened to block Warsh's nomination unless the investigation was dropped. White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt said Tillis should "do the right thing" and confirm Warsh quickly. In other news, a federal appeals court ruled that President Trump's executive order banning asylum claims at the southern border is illegal. The court said people have the right to apply for asylum. The FDA also said it will fast-track reviews for three psychedelic drugs (drugs that change how you see and feel) to treat mental health conditions like depression and PTSD. Two of the drugs come from magic mushrooms, and one is based on MDMA (Ecstasy). And a powerful tornado hit northern Oklahoma, destroying dozens of homes. Amazingly, no one died—only minor injuries were reported.
why_it_matters: This story shows how different parts of the government make decisions that affect our money, our safety, and what medicines might be available in the future — it's like seeing how the country's leaders solve big problems.

keywords.explanation: The part of the U.S. government that enforces laws and runs criminal investigations.
The central bank that manages the U.S. money supply and interest rates.
President Trump's nominee to become the next chair of the Federal Reserve.
Protection given by a country to someone who has fled their own country because of danger.
The U.S. Food and Drug Administration, which decides if new medicines are safe.
A powerful, spinning wind storm that can destroy buildings and homes.

questions.question: What did the Justice Department drop?
options: A criminal investigation into Jerome Powell
questions.correct_answer: A criminal investigation into Jerome Powell

questions.question: Who will now look into the cost overruns at the Fed?
Why did Senator Thom Tillis threaten to block Kevin Warsh's nomination?
options: Because the DOJ hadn't dropped the investigation
questions.correct_answer: Because the DOJ hadn't dropped the investigation

questions.question: What did the federal appeals court say about Trump's asylum ban?
Which drugs did the FDA decide to fast-track?
What part of the U.S. was hit by a powerful tornado?

background_read: The Federal Reserve is like the bank for all U.S. banks and helps keep the economy stable.
When the head of the Fed is investigated, it can affect how people trust the country's money system.
Tornadoes are measured by how fast their winds spin, and they can be very dangerous.
Article_Structure: WHO: Jerome Powell (Fed Chair), Kevin Warsh, Senator Thom Tillis, Karoline Leavitt
WHAT: Justice Department drops criminal probe into Powell; court blocks asylum ban; FDA fast-tracks psychedelics; tornado hits Oklahoma
WHERE: Washington D.C. (DOJ, Fed, court, FDA) and northern Oklahoma (tornado)
WHY: To clear the way for Warsh's confirmation; because the asylum ban was ruled illegal; to speed up mental health treatments; natural disaster

perspectives.description: They think dropping the investigation was necessary to get a new Fed chair who will lower interest rates and help the economy.
They are happy that the appeals court blocked the asylum ban because they believe people fleeing danger should have a fair chance to ask for protection.
They are relieved that no one died in the Oklahoma tornado, but they are concerned about the destruction and hope for better warning systems.
